channel.title: Recent & Upcoming Talks | Casper van Elteren

item.title: Through the looking glass
item.description: Since the birth of classical physics, we have come to regard physics as the asnwer to our question on what reality “is”. Yet many systems we experience every day devies the traditional laws of physics. These so-called complex systems are crucial to the functioning of the world around us; ranging from the creation of life and the inner-workings of cells, the surivival and propagation of ecosystems, the creation of the mind from neural interactions, to the social structures of society, and so on. In this talk, I will explore the relationships between the parts and whole of a system, and unravel insights in how systemic properties are created through local interactions. I will view complex systems through the lens of information theory and show novel methods to determine driver nodes in complex systems, and how tipping points may be understood from an information theoretical perspective.

item.title: What node is most important?
item.description: I was a guest speaker as part of the course Modeling and Data Analysis in Complex Networks. Additionally, the same slides were shown at the simulation based science group at the institute for advanced studies (IAS) at the university of Amsterdam. The talk concerned my research on the identification of driver-nodes in complex dynamical systems.
